At 0655 B/8, a convoy of three 3000-tons merchant vessels with an escorting Spica-class torpedo boat was seen three miles from the shore. The convoy was northbound an all three merchant vessels were subsequently seen to be in ballast. Lt. Stevens decided not to attack. The leading merchant vessel was camouflaged blue and grey, the remainder were painted grey.
At 0730 B/8, P 46 commenced reconnoitring the coast for a night gun attack on the coastal railway.
At 1200 B/2, HMS P 46 had found a suitable position for the night gun action in position 39°04'N, 16°05'E. The submarine then patrolled in the vicinity keeping about three miles from the shore.
At 2000 B/2, HMS P 46 surfaced. No trains passed until 2130 hours when one southbound train was observed. A dummy run on this train was carried out by the gunlayer and trainer.

At 2230 B/2, the light of a southbound train was sighted and P 46 commenced to close the coast but she was not close enough to open fire so remained waiting closer to the coast for the next southbound train.
Northbound trains were relatively frequent but the two southbound trains that passed were very small and were not attacked. (5)
9 Oct 1942 HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN), bombarded a southbound train near position 39°04'N, 16°05'30"E.
Italian sources confirm that a military train was shelled near Nocera Terinese but there were no damages or casualties.
0200 B/9 - Sighted the lights of a southbound train approaching.

0210 B/9 - Opened fire with the 3" gun. Nine rounds were fired for two hits. The train was composed of eight passenger coaches (besides the engine) and was seen to stop about half a mile south of the attack. The first hit, which was on the second or third coach, extinguished the lights of the train.
0215 B/9 - Unfortunately the train was not destroyed but P 46 withdrew to the north-ward to charge the battery.
0855 B/9 - Proceeded dived to northwards keeping three miles from the coast. (5)

11 Oct 1942 HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian transport Una (1395 GRT, built 1904) near Capri Island. Two men were missing, forty-five survivors were picked up including fourteen wounded.

0235 B/11 - Sighted a small darkened vessel stopped in position 180° - Monaco Point (Capri) - 5 miles. Considered this to be an A/S schooner on patrol. Evaded by proceeding to the westward on the surface.
0425 B/11 - Sighted a small darkened vessel proceeding to the north-west.
0600 B/11 - Patrolled in the vicinity of position 180° - Canpanella Point - 5 miles.
0810 B/11 - Sighted masts and funnel of a ship on a southerly course to the northward of Bocca Piccola. Decided to proceed to the west of Capri to intercept her.
0840 B/11 - Started attack.
0904 B/11 - In position 40°29'N, 14°15'E fired three torpedoes from 4000 yards. All missed ahead.
0909 B/11 - The enemy altered course to port, presumably to return to harbour.
0913 B/11 - Heard three explosions at the end of run of the torpedoes.
0921 B/11 - Started a second attack, we had one torpedo left in the tubes.

0932 B/11 - In position 40°30'N, 14°15E fired one torpedo from 1400 yards. The torpedo hit just forward of the funnel. The ship was seen to burst into flames. P 46 meanwhile went deep and retired to the southwest as aircraft were seen to approach.

1035 B/11 - Returned to periscope depth. The target was not sighted but a trawler type vessel was seen to be stopped in the attack area, presumably picking up survivors. A Cant aircraft was seen searching the area.
1410 B/11 - In position 40°22'N, 14°15'E, sighted a southbound hospital ship.
1500 B/11 - Sighted a Spica-class torpedo boat, evidently hunting the submarine. Proceeded slowly to the south-west to evade.
1600 B/11 - Two destroyers / torpedo boats were now in sight.

1720 B/11 - One of the destroyers / torpedo boats closed while sweeping with her Asdic. She was proceeding dead slow and passed 3000 yards astern. After some time she left the area. She was never in contact with the submarine.
1800 B/11 - Both destroyer / torpedo boats were now out of sight.
2000 B/11 - Surfaced and set course for Cape Gallo, Sicily.
2025 B/11 - Jettisoned a dummy persicope in position 40°13'N, 14°03'E.
2100 B/11 - Dived on seeing a darkened vessel ahead.
2140 B/11 - Surfaced and continued passage to the south-south-west.
2147 B/11 - Picked up fast turbine HE on the port quarter but nothing was sighted. P 46 dived to avoid being sighted.
2158 B/11 - The HE faded out to the north-east.
2235 B/11 - Surfaced and continued passage to the south-south-west. (5)

13 Oct 1942 HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian transport Loreto (1055 GRT, built 1912) off Cape Gallo, Sicily, Italy in position 38°14'N, 13°14'E. 129 Indian POW's aboard and 18 of the Italian crew were lost from a total of 400 and 57 respectively.
0030 B/13 - Commenced patrol off Cape Gallo.
1640 B/13 - Sighted masts and funnels of an eastbound ship keeping close inshore.
1720 B/13 - Started attack. The target was a merchant vessel of 1500 tons. A gun was seen aft and she was in ballast.
1733 B/13 - In position 38°14'N, 13°14'E fired three torpedoes from 1150 yards. Two hits were obtained.
1745 B/13 - The ship was seen to sink by the stern. Withdrew to the north-west.
2210 B/13 - Passed the signal timed 1705 B/13 in which it was reported P 46 was returning to Malta E.T.A. at 1000 B/16. (5)
15 Oct 1942 At 0530 B/15, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) commenced the eastbound passage of Q.B.B. 255 (mined area) at 120 feet. (5)
16 Oct 1942 Around 1920 B/16, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) ended her 6th war patrol (5th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was swept in by HMS Speedy (Lt. J.G. Brookes, DSC, RN) which had joined around 1700 B/16. (5)
31 Oct 1942 HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) is docked in No.1 graving dock at the Malta Dockyard. (21)
1 Nov 1942 HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) is undocked. (22)

2 Nov 1942 At 1000 A/2, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 7th war patrol (6th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the North of Sicily, off Cape San Vito, to provide cover during the Allied landings in North Africa (Operation Torch).
She departed Malta together with HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN) and HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN). They were escorted out until around 1730 A/2 by HMS Speedy (Lt. J.G. Brookes, DSC, RN). (5)
2 Nov 1942 For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 46 during her 7th war patrol see the map below.
3 Nov 1942 At 0530 A/3, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) dived to make the westbound passage through Q.B.B. 255 (mined area) at 120 feet.
At 1934 A/3, surfaced and set course for her patrol position off Cape San Vito. (5)
4 Nov 1942 At 0550 A/4, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) dived in the vicinity of Cape San Vito and patrolled to the eastward.
At 0745 A/4, north-west of Cape San Vito, in approximate position 38°15'N, 12°37'E, sighted a small westbound merchant vessel close inshore.
At 0810 A/4, a Spica-class torpedo boat was sighted proceeding eastwards.
At 0940 A/4, a Leone-class destroyer was seen proceeding eastwards. [note: all Leone-class destroyers had been lost in the Red Sea in 1941.]
At 1030 A/4, three minesweepers were seen proceeding to the north-westwards on course 150°.
At 1050 A/4, the minesweepers were seen to turn to the southwards in position 38°14'N, 12°42'E.
At 1430 A/4, two Abba-class torpedo boats were seen to proceed to the westward.

At 1855 A/4, HMS P 46 surfaced and proceeded to close Palermo to comply with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1831 B/1 in which P 46 was ordered to investigate enemy night patrols in that area 4 to 8 miles from the Palermo breakwater. [This was presumably for a planned upcoming Chariot operation against Palermo harbour.]
At 2105 A/4, two darkened vessels were sighted rounding Cape Gallo and proceeding to the north-west. This were thought to be torpedo boats.
Between 2130 A/4 and 0030 A/5, HMS P 46 patrolled at slow speed with the main engines stopped about 5 to 7 miles from the Palermo breakwater. Nothing was sighted and nothing was heard on the Asdic.
At 0030 A/5, HMS P 46 set course to return to patrol off Cape San Vito. (5)

5 Nov 1942 At 0330 A/5, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) sighted a fast motor boat proceeding eastwards while keeping close inshore. Course was altered away to evade until the enemy vessel was out of sight.

At 0518 A/5, HMS P 46 dived and patrolled to the north-west of Cape San Vito in the vicinity of position 38°13'N, 12°41'E [the patrol reports gives this position as 38°13'N, 12°14'E but this must be a mistake, probably a typing error. The position as given by us must be the correct position and corresponds more or less with the 0800 and 1200 position in the logbook.]
At 0605 A/5, three tugs were seen towing a large dredger and one lighter. They were eastbound and keeping close inshore.
At 0810 A/5, a small motor yacht with Red Cross makings passed proceeded eastwards.
At 0944 A/5, two Generali-class torpedo boats were seen proceeding westwards close inshore.
At 1230 A/5, an eastbound merchant vessel of about 3000-tons was sighted. This vessel was in ballast.
At 1310 A/5, two motor torpedo boats were seen proceeding eastwards keeping close inshore. (5)

6 Nov 1942 HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) attacked an Italian submarine with four torpedoes north of Cape San Vito, Sicily. All torpedoes missed. This was most probably Bronzo on her way to Trapani but the attack was unobserved. Another submarine (probably Avorio) was also observed at 1300 hours but was too far to be attacked.
0658 A/6 - Sighted a south-west bound submarine. Started attack.
0708 A/6 - In position 38°16'N, 12°43'E fired four torpedoes from 3000 yards at a submarine which was thought to be Italian. No hits were obtained.
1120 A/6 - Sighted a Generale-class torpedo boat passing close astern proceeding northwards.
1150 A/6 - Sighted a destroyer approaching bow on at high speed from the eastwards. Went deep.
1218 A/6 - HE of this destroyer faded astern. Returned to periscope depth. Both the vessel seen at 1120 and 1150 hours were now seen to be well clear.
1300 A/6 - Sighted a submarine to the westward proceeding to the south-west. Range was 6000 yards. Endeavoured to close. Unable to identify the submarine type but it seemed to be a fairly large one.
1340 A/6 - Abandoned the chase.

1445 A/6 - Sighted a 3000-tons tanker escorted by two Generale-class torpedo boats passing 4000 yards to the north on a south-west course. No attack was made as a full salvo needed to be retained for enemy warships likely to be encountered in the opening stages of operation Torch.
1550 A/6 - Sighted two Generale-class torpedo boats proceeding westwards close inshore. They were proceeding at high speed. (5)

7 Nov 1942 At 0350 A/7, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) sighted a destroyer / torpedo boat type vessel silhouetted against a searchlight beam to the westward. The enemy vessel was on a southerly course.

At 0800 A/7, west-north-west of Cape San Vito, in position 38°12'N, 12°39'E, sighted a vessel similar to a cross channel steamer proceeding eastwards close inshore. A gun was mounted forward on this vessel.

At 0905 A/7, a convoy of one tanker and a 2000-tons merchant vessel, both in ballast and escorted by a torpedo boat (Orsa-class). They passed close to the submarine on a course of 012°. The torpedo boat was in contact with her Asdic for at least 5 minutes but no attack developed.

At 1855 A/7, a darkened ship was seen closing from the east. P 46 dived to avoid detection. Engine HE was picked up at 185 revs which eventually faded out to the south-west. P 46 surfaced a little over 20 minutes later. (5)

8 Nov 1942 HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) torpedoed and damaged the Italian light cruiser Attilio Regolo off Cape San Vito, Sicily in position 38°14'N, 12°43'E. The cruiser lost its bow nearly up to the bridge. Lt. Stevens could not finish off the ship since he ran out of torpedoes.

The destroyers Nicolò Zeno and Antonio da Noli hunted the submarine. The damaged cruiser was towed to port by the tug Polifemo escorted by the torpedo boats Cigno, Lince and Giuseppe Cesare Abba. Another attack later the same day by HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) failed.

When Attilio Regolo was torpedoed by P 46 she was returning from a milelaying mission. She was in company with the Italian destroyers Antonio Pigafetta, Antonio da Noli, Nicolò Zeno, Corraziere, Ascari and Mitragliere. They had laid minefield 'S 8' off Cape Bon except Corraziere which had been detached to lay minefield 'ST 2' in an adjacent area.
0815 A/8 - In position 38°12'N, 12°39'E, sighted a large 'ocean going' tug towing a small schooner which passed close inshore proceeding eastwards.

0953 A/8 - Received an enemy report from an aircraft timed 0840 A/8. This reported a light cruiser and six destroyers approaching from the south-west at 12 knots. It was estimated that these ships should pass P 46's position at approximately 1140 hours.
0955 A/8 - Sighted masts and smoke with aircraft overhead to the south-west. This was most likely the cruiser and destroyers we had just been warned about.
1000 A/8 - Sighted the director tower of a cruiser to the south-west.
1005 A/8 - Commenced to close at full speed for an attack.

1023 A/8 - In position 38°14'N, 12°43'E fired a full salvo of four torpedoes from a range of 2100 yards but probably less. One torpedo was heard to hit but the result could not be observed as P 46 meanwhile had gone deep to evade the strong escort of destroyers and aircraft.
1035 A/8 - Depth charging started. 14 single charges were dropped during the next hour but none were close.
1245 A/8 - Came to periscope depth. Destroyers were seen to be circling near the attack area. Decided to retire to the west.

1428 A/8 - Surfaced and passed an enemy report timed 1023 A/8 which contained the following; One torpedo hit on 6-inch cruiser. At 1245 A/8 probably stopped inside smoke screen position 38°14'N, 12°41'E. All torpedoes expended. Intend returning through Q.B.B. 255 daylight 9th.
1445 A/8 - Dived and set course to return to Malta as all torpedoes had been expended. (23)
9 Nov 1942 At 0515 A/9, P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) commenced the eastbound passage of Q.B.B. 255 (mined area) at 120 feet. (5)
11 Nov 1942 Around 1250 A/11, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) ended her 7th war patrol (6th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was escorted in by HMS Hebe (Lt.Cdr. G. Mowatt, RD, RNR) which had joined around 1000 A/11. (24)
16 Nov 1942 At 1200 A/16, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 8th war patrol (7th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Gulf of Sirte.
She was escorted out until 1415 A/16 by HMS Rye (Lt. J.A. Pearson, DSC and Bar, RNR).

At 1330 A/16, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1313 A/16 was received by V/S which ordered P 46 to patrol in the vicinity of Lampion Island with despatch and that further orders would follow. Course was set accordingly when P 46 left the swept channel at 1415 hours.

In a signal timed 1823 A/16, the Capt. S.10 sent the following; Establish patrol in the vicinity of position 190° - Lampion Island - 12 miles. One large merchant ship and two in position 38°30'N, 12°20'E, course 180°, speed 9 at 1240 A/16. After attack or at 1800 A/17 proceed with despatch on the surface by day at your discretion to patrol within 20 miles to the north and east of Sirte. HMS Porpoise (Lt. L.W.A. Bennington, DSC, RN) patrolling to the north-west of Misurata is aware of your movements.

The reported convoy was made up of the Italian tanker Sirio (5222 GRT, built 1921) escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Ardito and Castelfidardo. The tanker was on passage from Naples to Tripoli. (5)
16 Nov 1942 For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 46 during her 8th war patrol see the map below.

17 Nov 1942 In a signal timed 1323 A/17, the Capt. S.10 sent the following to HMS Porpoise (Lt. L.W.A. Bennington, DSC, RN), HMS P 212 (Lt. J.H. Bromage, DSC, RN) and HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN); One merchant ship and two destroyers in position 33°50'N, 17°04'E, course 220°, speed 10 knots at 0940 A/17.

This must have referred to the convoy made up of the Italian tanker Giulio Giordani (10534 GRT, built 1939) escorted by the Italian destroyers Bersagliere and Granatiere. They were on passage from Taranto to Libya. (25)
17 Nov 1942 At 0300 A/17, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) established patrol in position 190° - Lampion Island - 12 miles.

Nothing was sighted of the expected convoy and at 1800 A/17, course was set to proceed on the surface by day to the vicinity of Sirte in accordance with the orders in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1823 A/16. (5)
19 Nov 1942 At 0700 A/19, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) arrived in her patrol area, within 20 miles north and east of Sirte.
On the 19th and 20th, the heavy sea made it impossible to keep an effective patrol at periscope depth. P 46 therefore patrolled deep, only coming up for W/T routines and periscope observations. (5)

21 Nov 1942 Between 1600 A/21 and 1700 A/21, nine motor landing craft were observed proceeding westwards in groups of three. The had apparently called at Sirte for stores and / or orders as the were still laden on departure. One was seen to be wearing the Italian flag and had the marking MZ 759 painted on her hull. (5)
22 Nov 1942 HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) damaged the Italian tug Porto Fossone (89 GRT) with gunfire off Sirte, Libya. The vessel was only slightly damaged; one man was wounded.
0145 A/22 - Sighted a darkened westbound vessel closing from the eastward. This was later identified as a small tug.

0210 A/22 - In position 31°19'N, 16°38'E opened fire with the 3" gun from 1000 yards. Fired 45 rounds for about 5 hits. The hits appeared to cause only superficial damage as the enemy was able to head towards the shore at 9 knots. The gun action did not go as planned due to difficulties with the gun.
0230 A/22 - Withdrew to the northward.
0900 A/22 - A small tug, most likely the one engaged during the night, was seen in Sirte roads.

2300 A/22 - Set course for the Buerat area to comply with the orders in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1400 A/21 which contained the following orders for P 46; Leave your current area at 2330 A/22 by the coastal route to establish patrol off Buerat by dawn/23. In view of moon condition submarines on coastal route must keep outside visibility distance from the shore. (5)

23 Nov 1942 At 0430 A/23, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) was in position 8 miles bearing 045° from the Buerat lighthouse, proceeding at slow speed on the motors and listening. This was in accordance with the orders as given in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1232 A/22 [We have been unable to find the text of this signal.]

At 1105 A/23, a small tug was seen approaching from the south-east. This looked like the same tug that had been seen off Sirte the day before and that had been engaged with the gun during the night of 21/22 November.
At 1215 A/23, the tug altered course to proceed to the Buerat anchorage where she stopped. Other vessels seen in the anchorage were one other tug, one schooner and two or three lighters.

At 2130 B/23, four darkened vessels were seen to the southwest proceeding northwards from the vicinity of the Buerat light buoy. These vessels were all small, three appearing to be tugs or trawlers, the other vessel was a motor boat. (5)

24 Nov 1942 At 0120 A/24, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) sighted a small darkened vessel proceeding dead slow or stopped inshore. Probably a motor boat type. Course was altered to evade this vessel until it disappeared.
At 0900 A/24, a periscope reconnaissance of the harbour revealed that only the small tug seen before was present. (5)

24 Nov 1942 In a signal 2015 A/24, the Capt. S.10 sent the following to HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN); If position compromised by attack of HMS P 44 (Lt. T.E. Barlow, RN) is to move to operate close to the east of Taguermess. Dawn is the important time for you to be close off Buerat returning as circumstances dictate at other times. (25)
25 Nov 1942 At 1130 A/25, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) sighted the small tug leaving Buerat and proceeding to the north-west keeping close to the coast.

At 1943 A/25, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1911 A/25 was received which reported that one merchant vessel, one torpedo boat and two landing craft were approaching. [We have been unable to find the exact text of this signal.]

This probably referred to the convoy made up of the Italian transport Algerino (977 GRT, built 1921) and the German F-lighters (landing barges) F 350 and F 540 which were on passage from Tripoli to Buerat escorted by the Italian auxiliary patrol vessel Eso (former Yugoslavian minesweeper Sokol). The convoy was attacked by aircraft around 1000 B/25 and the Algerino was damaged and beached to the east of Misurata. She thus was no longer with the convoy which arrived at Buerat around 0800 A/26.
Lt. Stevens decided to patrol in position 360° - Beurat lighthouse - 5.5 miles. This position was selected to cover the approach to Buerat either inside or outside the Hissa Bank.
The following day HMS P 46 sighted nothing though. Visibility was about 4 miles on the 26th. (5)
27 Nov 1942 At 1800 A/27, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) set course to return to Malta keeping four miles from the shore as far north as Misurata.

This was in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2330 A/26 which had contained the following; You have freedom of action in your area, leave for Malta after dark/27 proceeding on the surface at your discretion to make rendezvous with the minesweeper at 1302 A/29. Be close off the Buerat light buoy dawn tomorrow. (26)
29 Nov 1942 Around 1630 A/29, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) ended her 8th war patrol (7th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was escorted in by HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN) which had joined around 1245 A/29. (27)
10 Dec 1942 At 1300 A/10, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 9th war patrol (8th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol north of Tunisia.
Around 1500 A/10, HMS Parthian (Lt. M.B. St. John, RN), which had sailed at 1345 A/10, caught up.
The submarines were escorted out until shortly before 1600 A/10 by HMS Hythe (Lt.Cdr. L.B. Miller, RN). (5)
10 Dec 1942 For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 46 during her 9th war patrol see the map below.
11 Dec 1942 At 0556 A/11, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) dived to make the westbound passage through Q.B.B. 255 (mined area).
At 1750 A/11, HMS P 46 surfaced having succesfully completed the passage of Q.B.B. 255. (27)
12 Dec 1942 At 0100 A/12, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) commenced to patrol in position 37°42'N, 10°50'E.

At 1515 A/12, a large motor vessel with an escort of three torpedo boats were seen proceeding to the north-east. The target could only be seen intermittently due to the many rain showers. Lt. Stevens endeavoured to close for attack but at 1559 A/12 the vessel was seen for the last time.
[Caterina Costa en-route from Bizerta to Naples ???]
At 1656 A/12, two motor boats were seen in line ahead eastbound. The look similar to the British Motor Launches (ML's).
At 2025 A/12, a torpedo boat was seen proceeding eastwards. The enemy vessel was approaching from ahead and P 46 dived until the torpedo boat had passed clear.

At 2320 A/12, HE was picked up on bearing 110° and a destroyer was seen passing to the north-east. Lt. Stanley turned stern on. Course was altered to proceed to patrol in position 37°32'N, 10°30'E as had been ordered in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1918 A/12. (28)
13 Dec 1942 At 0217 A/13, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) dived for a destroyer closing from the westward.
At 1952 A/13, in position 37°32'N, 10°33'E, HMS P 46 dived for two motor torpedo boats seen closing from the westwards. (28)

14 Dec 1942 HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian transport Castelverde (6958 GRT, built 1921) about 30 nautical miles north-north-west of Cape Bon, Tunisia in position 37°29'N, 10°46'E. Fifteen men were killed or missing, 248 survivors were picked up.

Castelverde had been in convoy together with the Italian transport Honestas (4960 GRT, built 1920). They were escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Ardito and Fortunale. About three hours earlier, the convoy had been attacked without success by HMS P 228 (Lt. I.L.M. McGeogh, RN) and just a few minutes before HMS P 212 (Lt. J.H. Bromage, DSC, RN) had sunk Honestas. The convoy was on passage from Napels to Tunis.

0700 A/14 - A land fix showed that P 46 had been set to the north-east during the night and that we were 10 miles from our patrol position. Closed the Bizerta convoy route and proceeded via it to our patrol position.
1106 A/14 - Sighted aircraft circling bearing 060°. Thought that this means a convoy approaching. Altered course to intercept.
1300 A/14 - Sighted the funnel of a merchant vessel. Three aircraft were seen circling overhead.
1315 A/14 - The convoy was now seen to be made up of two merchant vessels in line ahead. They were escorted by two torpedo boats and were bound for Tunis.
1351 A/14 - The leading ship was torpedoed by another submarine. The second ship altered towards but soon resumed her original course.

1403 A/14 - In position 37°29'N, 10°46'E fired three torpedoes at this merchant vessel from 2000 yards. The third torpedo broke surface and ran off to port, possibly a gyro failure. One hit was obtained, the torpedo hit just abaft the funnel. P 46 then went deep and turned stern on the escorts.

1428 A/14 - A pattern of four depth charges was dropped fairly close. P 46 was now hunted for about half an hour in which 27 depth charges were dropped, several fairly close. They broke a few lamps but caused no other damage.
1533 A/14 - Returned to periscope depth. Both ships were seen to be stopped. The torpedo boats were close by the targets.
1553 A/14 - In position 37°30'N, 10°46'E fired one torpedo at 'our' target from 6000 yards. The target was seen to be down by the stern.
1555 A/14 - An aircraft was seen to drop flares over the torpedo track. Went deep and took avoiding action.
1557 A/14 - Heard the torpedo hit the target. This was followed by breaking up noises.
1600 A/14 - A depth charge was dropped. More followed but none were close.
1720 A/14 - Returned to periscope depth. 'Our' target was not seen but the other ship was seen to be burning fiercely.
1735 A/14 - Heard a very violent explosion. Only smoke could be seen where the ship had been.
1917 A/14 - Surfaced and set course to regain patrol position. (5)

15 Dec 1942 HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) sank the Italian transport Sant'Antioco (4994 GRT, built 1919) about 35 nautical miles north-north-west of Cape Bon, Tunisia in position 37°37'N, 10°44'E. Twenty-nine were killed or missing, over 200 survivors were rescued.

Sant'Antioco was in convoy with the German transport Brott (former Norwegian, then French, 1583 GRT, built 1937). [Brott had her engine aft and thus looked like a tanker.] They were escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Orione and Groppo. The convoy was on passage from Naples to Bizerta.
0640 A/15 - Heard HE on the port quarter. On coming to periscope depth two destroyers were seen passing close astern on a north-east course. No Asdic transmissions were heard.
0926 A/15 - Heard a distant heavy explosion.
1050 A/15 - Aircraft were seen circling on the horizon bearing bearing 050°. Altered course to close.

1240 A/15 - Sighted two merchant vessels approaching on a course for Bizerta. These two ships were later seen to be a 4000-ton merchant vessel and a medium sized tanker. They were escorted by two torpedo boats.
1330 A/15 - In position 37°32'N, 10°39'E fired four torpedoes at the 4000-ton merchant ship. Range was 4000 yards.
1333 A/15 - Heard two timed torpedo explosions. The result could not be observed as P 46 had gone deep.
1336 A/15 - A depth charge was dropped. This was followed by a pair of charges and then another single one.
1406 A/15 - A pattern of 12 depth charges was dropped very close causing some minor damage. The hunt continued until about 1830 hours. By then a total of 62 depth charges were counted.

2008 A/15 - Surfaced and withdrew to the north-east subsequently setting course to proceed through Q.B.B. 255 (mined area) by the route to the south of Pantelleria to Malta as all torpedoes had been expended. (5)

15 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 1043 A/15, the Capt. S.10 sent the following to HMS United (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN), HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN), HMS P 212 (Lt. J.H. Bromage, DSC, RN), HMS P 228 (Lt. I.L.M. McGeogh, RN) and HMS P 247 (Lt. M.G.R. Lumby, DSC, RN); Reconnaissance shows success in your area. You are doing splendidly, naturally the enemy like to hunt you with all available forces though these not numerous. Enemy traffic must continue down channel where you are disposed and this is expected in immediate future. You should therefore avoid detection and endeavour to maintain position. Do not read special routines if this jeopardises security, maintain W/T silence under all circumstances. If hard pressed withdraw to north or east by safe route, returning when enemy shaken off. (29)
17 Dec 1942 At 0100 A/17, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) sighted one or possibly two unidentified small vessel off Linosa Island.
Around 1130 A/17, HMS P 46 ended her 9th war patrol (8th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (5)

28 Dec 1942 Around 2015 A/28, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 10th war patrol (9th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to perform a special operation (pick up Chariot personnel after the attack on Palermo harbour).

30 Dec 1942 At 1817 A/30, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) surfaced in position 37°54'N, 11°21'E in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1055 A/30. Course was then set to proceed towards Ustica.

The signal timed 1055 A/30 had contained the following orders for HMS P 46; Endeavour to be in position 37°54'N, 11°21'E at sunset tonight, then steer direct toward Ustica. You must expect a torpedo boat patrol on this line and also targets about midnight.

At 2100 A/30, two darkened vessels were sighted to the southward. These vessels were westbound and were probably torpedo boats. Course was alter so that the submarine was stern on. The two vessels later altered course to the eastward and were finally lost from sight 40 minutes after they were first sighted.
At 2320 A/30, red flared were observed to the south-west. (28)

30 Dec 1942 In a signal timed 2209 A/30, the Capt. S.10 sent the following information to HMS Ursula (Lt. R.B. Lakin, DSC, RN), HMS P 37 (Lt. E.T. Stanley, DSC, RN) and HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN); Aircraft are patrolling your area tonight after moonrise in endeavour to bomb enemy torpedo boats but enemy aircraft must be expected hunting you. (29)

31 Dec 1942 Between 0215 A/31 and 0400 A/31, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) intermittently sighted a torpedo boat patrolling of Marittimo. Nothing was however seen of the expected traffic that had been reported in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1055 A/30. The red flares sighted at 2320 A/30 may indicate that it passed to the southward.
From dawn, HMS P 46 patrolled in the vicinity of position 360° - Cape San Vito - 10 miles.
At 1125 A/31, masts of an eastbound merchant vessel were seen close inshore off Cape San Vito.
At 1830 A/31, HMS P 46 set course for the waiting position off Ustica Island. (28)
1 Jan 1943 At 0200 A/1, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) commenced to patrol in the vicinity of position 060° - Ustica Falconiera - 7 miles.
At 1200 A/1, HMS P 46 commenced to patrol while staying deep only coming up of W/T routines. (28)
2 Jan 1943 At 2100 A/2, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) left the waiting position off Ustica to proceed to the rendezvous point with the Charioteers of Operation Principal. (28)

3 Jan 1943 In position 3.5 miles - Diga Fonanea lighthouse - 044°, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN), picked up the crew of two from Chariot No. XXII that had been launched by HMS Trooper (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSO, DSC, RN). They had to abandon their attempt to enter Palermo harbour.

0211 A/3 - The Asdic-operator reported A/S transmissions bearing 340°. P 46 dived. No HE was picked up and nothing could be seen through the periscope. The transmissions were irregular and ceased at 0220 A/3.
0250 A/3 - Surfaced and a fix showed the position of the submarines as being 2 miles 120° from the ordered pick-up position. Course was adjusted to close the pick-up position on the main motors.

0350 A/3 - A white light was thought to be seen flashing bearing 230° and a few minutes later this was confirmed as flashing "X's" which were the prearranged signal. The light was kept on a steady bearing but seemed to take a long time to get closer.
0425 A/3 - No other lights had been sighted and there being no evidence of any patrol activity course was altered to 235° and speed was increased to 8 knots to close the Chariot.
0430 A/3 - The Chariot's light was close on the starboard bow. The submarine was stopped and the Chariot was seen to close the submarine.

The crew of the Chariot (No. XXIII, Sub.Lt. H.L.H Stevens, RNVR and Leading Seaman Carter, boarded P 46 with a little assistance. They were in good health but reported not to have reached the harbour.
0438 A/3 - With no other Chariots in sight, HMS P 46 withdrew on the surface to the north-east at full speed.
1620 A/3 - Received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1203 A/3 [see the other event of 3 January 1943 for the text of this signal] and set course towards position 38°40'N, 12°40'E as had been ordered.
2140 A/3 - Passed P 46's signal timed 2005 A/3 to the Capt. S.10. The signal was cleared via Whitehall W/T. (30)

3 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 1203 A/3, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 43 (Lt. A.R. Daniell, DSC, RN), HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN), HMS P 311 (Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO and 2 Bars, RN), HMS Thunderbolt (Lt.Cdr. C.B. Crouch, DSO and Bar, RN) and HMS Trooper (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSO, DSC, RN); Take the following action; P 43 move to a waiting position 090° - Ustica - 6 miles (38°42'N, 13°24'E). P 46, if you have personnel on board withdraw to position 38°40'N, 12°40'E and signal report of their operation and yours in one time pad after dark. If you have no extra personnel proceed to position 38°30'N, 12°00'E and report after dark thence proceed via Cape Milazzo northward to operate to the south of Naples off the Bocca Piccolo. Thunderbolt set course to pass through position 38°50'N, 12°00'E to arrive in position 'G' (38°00'N, 11°25'E) at 0800 A/4. Thence by normal route to rendezvous with minesweepers at the end of the searched channel at 0800 A/6. Trooper to follow Thunderbolt's route 24 hours later to arrive at Malta at 0800 A/7. P 311 if transfer of Lt. Kerr allowed follows 24 hours behind Trooper to arrive Malta 0800 A/8. (31)
4 Jan 1943 At 0615 A/4, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0603 A/4 which ordered HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) to report. Apparently the signal timed 2005 A/3 had not been received at Malta.
At 0700 A/4, HMS P 46 surfaced and passed her signal 0650 A/4 via Whitehall W/T 15 minutes later.
At 0715 A/4, Dived and set course for Cape San Vito as were the orders if Chariot Personnel had been picked up.
At 1900 A/4, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1109 A/4 was received. P 46 then closed position 360° as had been ordered in this signal. (32)
5 Jan 1943 At 2200 A/5, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) left patrol to return to Malta. (5)
6 Jan 1943 At 1825 A/6, west-south-west of Marettimo, in position 37°51'N, 11°49'E, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) HE was picked up on bearing 245°.
P 46 was about to surface but now remained deep. The HE closed on a steady bearing at 150 revs. No Asdic transmissions were heard.
At 1858 A/6, a vessel passed overhead and it was followed by two more. The enemy proceeded on course 060°. HE faded out around 1940 A/6.
Further HE had been heard at 1850 A/6 and at 1928 A/6 two vessels crossed astern.
It is considered this were escort vessesl returning to Italy from Tunisia. (5)

7 Jan 1943 At 0043 A/7, while north-east of Cape Bon, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) sighted two bow waves ahead. P 46 dived to avoid being sighted. Fast diesel HE was picked up which eventually faded on course 270°. The vessels encountered were probably friendly motor torpedo boats which had been referred to by the Capt. S.10's in his signals timed 1157 A/6 and 2022 A/6.
These signals referred to HMMTB 316 and HMMTB 311 which were proceeding from Malta to Bone.
At 0110 A/7, HMS P 46 surfaced and proceeded via the ordered route through Q.B.B. 255 (mined area).
At 0525 A/7, HMS P 46 dived for a bow wave sighted astern. No HE was picked up. Continued the passage dived.

At 0926 A/7, north-west of Pantelleria, in position 36°53'N, 11°40'E, sighted four small vessels which were subsequently identified as three small and one large schooner. It is thought these may be auxiliary minesweepers. Enemy course was 280°. All flew the Italian flag.

At 1210 A/7, west of Pantelleria, in position 36°48'N, 11°43'E, HE was heard at 250 revs. on bearing Red 50°. Nothing was seen though. It was assumed this may be HMS P 35 (Lt. S.L.C. Maydon, RN). [This was not the case as this submarine was off Sousse at this time.]

At 2105 A/7, HMS P 46, which had surfaced at 1830 A/7, dived for two motor boats approaching from ahead. These passed by without incident and were probably friendly motor torpedo boats of whose presence P 46 had been warned.
The motor torpedo boats P 46 had been warned about were HMMTB 260 and HMMTB 264 which conducted an anti-shipping sweep between Kelibia and Hammamet during the night of 7/8 January 1943. (33)
8 Jan 1943 Around 1500 A/8, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) ended her 10th war patrol (9th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (30)
11 Jan 1943 HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) is docked in No.1 graving dock at the Malta Dockyard. (34)
16 Jan 1943 HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) is undocked. (34)
19 Jan 1943 At 1300 A/19, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 11th war patrol (10th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the east coast of Tunisia.
She was routed through positions 36°00'N, 14°00'E and 36°06'N, 12°10'E thence to patrol westward of line 340° from Kerkennah number 4 buoy and north of latitude 35°48'E.
For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 46 during this patrol see the map below.

20 Jan 1943 At 0006 A/20, in position 010° - Linosa lighthouse - 11 miles, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) sighted a darkened object bearing 340°. Course was altered towards. The vessel appeared to be a submarine on an easterly course, but moving very slowly. As HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) and HMS P 44 (Lt. J.C.Y. Roxburgh, DSC, RN) were eastbound for Malta this night, it was assumed that this was one of them.
At 0020 A/20, HMS P 46 dived after the other submarine had altered course towards. Attempts to exchange identity by S.S./T. were unsuccessful.
The other submarine was HMS Una which reported seeing P 46 at 0025 A/20 after which HMS P 46 was seen to dive.
At 0122 A/20, HMS P 46 surfaced and resumed her original course.
At 2350 A/20, HMS P 46 established patrol in the vicinity of Ras Mahmur. (35)

20 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 1103 A/20, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN), HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) and HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN); Establish patrol forthwith west of line 340° from Kerkennah as follows; 1) P 42 south of latitude 35°30'N. 2) P 45 north of P 42 and south of latitude 36°00'N. 3) P 46 north of P 45. (31)
21 Jan 1943 Between 0945 A/21 and 1000 A/21, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) heard about fifteen distant explosions, including one group of six which were thought to be a depth charge pattern.

At 1130 A/21, in approximate position 36°30'N, 10°56'E, one torpedo boat coming from the west passed 3000 yards inshore. She was then proceeding northwards at 15 knots. No Asdic transmissions were heard. (5)

21 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 1116 A/21, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 42 (Lt. A.C.G. Mars, DSO, RN), HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) and HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN); P 42 leave patrol after dark tonight and return to Malta through positions 35°01'N, 12°20'E and 35°02'N, 14°10'E to make rendezvous with the minesweeper at 0830 A/23. P 45 move forthwith north of latitude 36°00'N, keeping south of 36°15'N. P 46 keep north of 36°15'N. Three motor torpedo boats from Malta will be operating off Tunisian the coast between 35°00'N and 36°00'N. They are routed north of Linosa and have orders not to attack submarines.
The motor torpedo boats referred to in this signal were HMMTB 264 and HMMTB 307 (only two sailed instead of three). (31)

21 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 2257 A/21, the Capt. S.10 sent the following orders to HMS P 45 (Lt. H.B. Turner, RN) and HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN); From dawn/22, P 45 operate between 35°30'N and 36°00'N. P 46 operate north of 36°00'N. (31)
23 Jan 1943 HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) sank the Italian sailing vessel Amabile Carolina (89 GRT, built 1907) off Hammamet, Tunisia.
0329 A/23 - Sighted a darkened vessel. Closed it to investigate. It was seen to be a small vessel with an F-boat in company. No attack with the gun was made as the F-boat may be heavily armed.
0349 A/23 - Dived. Periscope visibility was good in the bright moonlight. The small vessel was now seen to be a tug and not worth the torpedo expenditure.
The vessels encountered were most likely the tug Cyclope which was towing the damaged F 150. They were on passage from Sousse (departed 1300 A/22) to Tunis (arrived 1700 A/23).
0422 A/23 - Surfaced and proceeded slowly to the westward along the north coast of the Gulf of Hammamet keeping three miles from the shore.
0915 A/23 - Sighted masts of a schooner closing from the eastward.

0954 A/23 - Surfaced in position 36°12'N, 10°37'E and engaged the schooner with gunfire from the 3" gun from a range of 2000 yards. 29 rounds were fired and about 10 hits were obtained. The crew abandoned ship almost immediately and made off for the shore. After about 10 rounds shore batteries opened fire.
1022 A/23 - Dived when enemy gunfire now came closer.

1048 A/23 - The schooner only showed signs of minor damage. An attempt to board her after surfacing again at 1026 hours was not possible as shore batteries opened fire again forcing P 46 to dive. So fired one torpedo from 1000 yards. The torpedo hit and the schooner disintegrated into a large cloud of smoke and flames. She was probably carrying petrol.
1100 A/23 - Dived and proceeded to the south-west, keeping fove miles from the coast.
1830 A/23 - Surfaced and set course for Ras Mahmur which was considered to be the best focal point to intercept enemy traffic. (5)
24 Jan 1943 At 1600 A/24, south-east of Ras Mahmur, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) heard fast HE to the north-west.
At 1610 A/24, two motor torpedo boats were sighted proceeding to the north-east keeping close inshore. (5)
25 Jan 1943 HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) torpedoed and sank the small Italian tanker Teodolinda (361 GRT, built 1925) off Hammamet, Tunisia.
1100 A/25 - Proceeded westwards at 3.5 knots to investigate an aircraft report (time of origin 0930 A/25) of a small merchant vessel stopped in position 36°22'00.0"N 10°35'00.0"E
1230 A/25 - Sighted a small tanker anchored 3 cables to the westward of Hammamet Fort, close inshore. She was seen to be in ballast.
1303 A/25 - In position 36°23'N, 10°36'E fired one torpedo from 1500 yards. This torpedo was seen to run under. It appeared the attack was not noticed by the enemy.

1325 A/25 - Fired another torpedo. It hit in the engine room. The vessel was seen to settle by the stern, her back appeared to be broken forward of the bridge. A motley crowd of Arabs, soldiers and ladies of the town gathered on the seaward side of the fort to watch the proceedings. After a while the periscope was taken under fire by shore batteries. P 46 went deep and retired from the area. (5)
26 Jan 1943 HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) sank the Italian naval auxiliary Z 90 / Redentore (46 GRT, built 1906) with gunfire near Hammamet, Tunisia.
0227 A/26 - Sighted a darkened vessel to the westward. Closed to investigate.

0322 A/26 - In position 36°21'N, 10°39'E opened fire with the 3" gun at a vessel which proved to be a heavily laden caique that was westbound. fourteen rounds were fired. It is thought 9 hits were obtained. The crew abonded ship at once.
0328 A/26 - Dived as it was thought another vessel was approaching from the west but no HE could be heard.
0404 A/26 - Surfaced and closed the caique. Sent over a boarding party of two. The ship was in a sinking condition.
0415 A/26 - Fired another five rounds into the stern from point blank range.
0430 A/26 - Withdrew to the east.
0615 A/26 - Set course to proceed westwards keeping three miles from the shore.
0845 A/26 - Set course to the south-west keeping three miles from the coast proceeding towards the vicinity of Kuriat.
2300 A/26 - Arrived in the Kuriat area. (5)

27 Jan 1943 At 0600 A/27, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) proceeded southwards keeping three to four miles from the coast to comply with the orders in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1736 A/26 in which P 46 was ordered to keep south of 36°00'N after sunset on the 27th. [We have been unable to find the text of this signal.]

At 1900 A/27, P 46 commenced to patrol in the vicinity of Ras Kaboudia. This position was selected by Lt. Stanley as several previous reports showed shipping entering and leaving the northern end of the Kerkennah Channel. (5)
28 Jan 1943 At 1320 A/28, in approximate position 35°18'N, 11°13'E, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) sighted three F-boats proceeding northwards.
At 2332 A/28, HMS P 46 dived for a possible motor torpedo boat approaching. After diving no HE was was heard. (5)

29 Jan 1943 At 0950 A/29, in approximate position 35°18'N, 11°11'E, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) sighted four southbound F-boats. They were flying German ensigns. Three of them were numbered 476, 846 and 479.
This must have been the German F 477, F 479, F 480 and F 546 which had departed Sousse around 1700 A/3 for Sfax where they arrived around 1700 A/4. (36)
30 Jan 1943 At 1000 A/30, in approximate position 35°17'N, 11°13'E, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) sighted three southbound F-boats.
This were most likely the German F 360, F 542 and the Italian MZ 760 which had departed Sousse around 0300 A/30 for Sfax where they arrived around 1600 A/30.
At 2321 A/30, the Asdic operator reported Diesel HE bearing 130°.
At 2325 A/30, three or four F-boats were sighted proceeding northwards.

This were most probably the German F 349, F 350 and the Italian MZ 739 and MZ 751 which had departed Sfax around 1200 A/30 for Sousse where they arrived around 0800 A/31. The Italian schooner Pasquale Costa (Italian, 198 GRT, built 1910) made the passage with these four F-boats but was apparently not sighted by P 46. (36)

31 Jan 1943 HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) torpedoed and sank the German transport Lisboa (1799 GRT, built 1911) about north of Sousse, Tunisia. Lisboa was escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Cigno and was on a trip from Trapani to Sousse.

Two aircraft reports, timed 0337 A/31 and 0401 A/31, indicated that a medium sized merchant vessel and one destroyer were making for Sousse at 8 knots from the vicinity of position 36°34'N, 11°40'E (south-west of Pantelleria).
0510 A/31 - Lt. Stanley decided to try to intercept this convoy which should be possible by proceeding on the surface at full speed until 1000 A/31.
1000 A/31 - Arrived in position 050° - Sousse breakwater light - 15 miles. Dived.

1145 A/31 - Sighted a HA shell burst bearing 315° and shortly afterwards seven explosions were heard. It was considered this might be the result of an air attack on the convoy if the were following the coastal route. So Lt. Stanley decided to close the coast at 4 knots to intercept.
1358 A/31 - Sighted smoke bearing 290°.
1405 A/31 - Identified the masts of one merchant vessel and one torpedo boat close inshore. They were southbound.
1428 A/31 - Commenced attack. The torpedo boat was zigzagging on the port bow of the merchant vessel which was about 2000 tons gross.

1502 A/31 - In position 35°54'N, 10°38'E fired four torpedoes from 6000 yards. One hit was heard after a little over 6 minutes after firing giving a running range of 8200 yards. P 46 had gone deep on firing.

1515 A/31 - Returned to periscope depth. The merchant vessel was stopped and on fire with the hull invisible. The torpedo boat was proceeding to seaward well clear of P 46. Two minutes later she dropped two depth charges.
1525 A/31 - Three aircraft were now seen patrolling the area. Went deep again.
1650 A/31 - Returned to periscope depth. All that could be seen was grey smoke from the merchant vessel.
1725 A/31 - Dense black smoke was now seen.

1820 A/31 - Heard a heavy explosion. P 46 meanwhile withdrew to the east and later set course to return to Malta in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1237 A/30 which had contained the following orders / information; Leave patrol after dark/31. and return Malta through positions 35°33'N, 12°07'E and 36°01'N, 12°37'E thence to usual rendezvous at 0830 A/2. HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) leaves 30th to operate in Gulf Hammamet routed to the north of you. (5)

31 Jan 1943 In a signal timed 1737 A/31, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) and HMS Una (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) that Allied motor torpedo boats [this were HMMTB 260, HMMTB 264, HMMTB 307, HMMTB 313] will be operating continuously in the Kuriat area from tonight 31st until 0100/4th. They were ordered not to attack submarines. (31)
2 Feb 1943 Around 1055 A/2, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) ended her 11th war patrol (10th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She, and HMS Rorqual (Lt.Cdr. L.W. Napier, RN), were escorted in from around 0830 A/2 by HMS Hebe (Lt.Cdr. G. Mowatt, RD, RNR). (5)
12 Feb 1943 At 1100 A/12, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 12th war patrol (11th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the Gulf of Hammamet, Tunisia.
She was route to the south of Linosa while on passage to her patrol area.
14 Feb 1943 At 1200 A/14, HMS P 46 (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) commenced patrolling at periscope depth to the south-east of Ras Mahmur. (5)

15 Feb 1943 At 2030 A/15, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1125 A/15 which contained the following orders; Move 23 miles 242° forthwith at 6 knots by dawn tomorrow.
Course was set accordingly.

At 2245 A/15, a darkened vessel was sighted close inshore. The vessel was proceeding to the south-west. The vessel was identified as a schooner but also three small craft were seen with it. These were thought to possible be motor torpedo boats. Decided not to attack as it was unlikely to be successful. Continued to proceed to the northward. (37)

16 Feb 1943 At 0450 A/15, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0355 A/16 which reported the following; Aircraft sighted at 0245 A/16, three merchant ships and six destroyers 20 miles to the north of Pantellaria proceeding on course 190° at 10 knots.
[Alcano convoy ???]
Single white flares were observed to the east through night and it was thought that these were dropped by aircraft to indicate the convoy's position but nothing was sighted all day.
At 1225 A/16, three distant explosions were heard.
At 1240 A/16, two distant explosions were heard.
At 1307 A/16, three distant explosions were heard.
At 1412 A/16, two distant heavy explosions were heard.

At 2053 A/16, Unruffled set course for Ras Mahmur in compliance with the Capt. S.10's orders as in his signal tomed 1046 A/16. [We have been unable to find the text of this signal.] Unruffled arrived back in her patrol area around 0630 A/17. (37)

18 Feb 1943 HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) attacked the Italian schooners L'Angelo Raffaelo (74 GRT, built 1938) and Nicolò lo Porto (71 GRT, built 1915) with a total of three torpedoes off Nabeul, Tunisia. The crews abandoned both schooners. That night wind increased to gale force and both schooners were wrecked on the beach.
0435 A/18 - Sighted seven F-boats / lighters close inshore proceeding to the north.

This were most likely the German F 349, F 350, F 362, F 541, F 542 and F 546 on passage from Sousse (departed around 2200 A/17) to Trapani (arrived around 1100 A/22 but after having made a stopover at Pantelleria).
0635 A/18 - Heard HE to the northward.

0700 A/18 - Sighted a large schooner in tow of one of eight F-boats / lighters. They were northbound and close inshore. The schooner appeared to be in ballast and as the submarine was unable to get within torpedo range, she was left unmolested.

This were most likely the Italian sailing vessel V 32S / Nettuno 3 (343 GRT, built 1942) and the Italian lading barges MZ 714, MZ 724, MZ 733, MZ 739, MZ 751 and MZ 753 on passage from Sfax (departed around 0900 A/17) to Trapani via Pantelleria (arrived at Trapani around ????). The Italian auxiliary minesweeper R 103 / Ardito (38 GRT, built 1939) had departed Sfax with them but may have returned.
0800 A/18 - Sighted five F-boats / lighters passing. They were proceeding to the north. All were wearing the Italian ensign.
This were most likely the Italian landing barges MZ 713, MZ 720, MZ 748, MZ 754 and MZ 760 on passage from Sfax (departed around 1100 A/18) for Trapani (arrived at Trapani around ????).
1335 A/18 - Sighted masts of two schooners off Nabeul. Closed to attack. Observed two large schooners to be anchored close inshore.
1418 A/18 - Shore batteries opened fire at the periscope. Range to the schooners was 1500 yards. In position 36°25'N, 10°45'E fired one torpedo at the left hand schooner.
1419 A/18 - Fired one torpedo at the right hand schooner.

Both torpedoes missed their targets. The crews of both schooners abandoned ship as soon as they saw the tracks. Unruffled then withdrew but it was quickly decided to go back and shoot one more torpedo.
1513 A/18 - Shore batteries again opened fire on the periscope. Fired another torpedo at the right hand schooner. It broke surface and ran off slightly to port and again the torpedo missed.
During the night a wind increased to gale force and this did not look good for the schooners with no crew onboard. The area was examined on the 22nd and two new wrecks were seen ashore. (5)

20 Feb 1943 At 2350 A/20, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2305 A/20 which contained the following orders; Move 33 miles 230° at 4 knots by dawn tomorrow/21.
This ordered Unruffled to patrol the convoy route to the north-west of Pantelleria by dawn tomorrow. Course was set accordingly. (37)

21 Feb 1943 HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) torpedoed and sank the German transport Baalbeck (former French, 2115 GRT, built 1923) about 18 nautical miles east-south-east of Cape Bon, Tunisia in position 36°56'N, 11°23'E. She was in convoy together with the German transport Charles le Borgne (former French, 1426 GRT, built 1920). They were escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Groppo and the Italian corvette Gabbiano.

0742 A/21 - Heard HE to the eastward and sighted a merchant vessel. This soon developed into a convoy of two merchant ships with two torpedo boats on each beam. Several aircraft were patrolling overhead.
0745 A/21 - Commenced attack.

0814 A/21 - In position 36°56'N, 11°23'E fired four torpedoes at the northernmost merchant vessel from 4000 yards. Almost three minutes after firing a loud explosion was heard followed shortly after a smaller explosion. This was followed by breaking up noises. Almost five minutes after firing another loud explosion was heard. Unruffled meanwhile had gone deep.
0842 A/21 - Depth charging commenced. A pattern of five was dropped reasonably close.
0930 A/21 - Returned to periscope depth. A torpedo boat could be seen which was only just visible. Nothing else was in sight. Unruffled now withdrew to the south.
2230 A/21 - Cleared the signal timed 2215 A/21 reporting the situation and one torpedo remaining. (5)
22 Feb 1943 At 2100 A/22, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) which had conducted a close periscope reconnaissance of Hammamet and Nabeul during the day, left patrol to return to Malta. (5)
24 Feb 1943 Around 1045 A/24, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSC, RN) ended her 12th war patrol (11th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She, and HMS Thunderbolt (Lt.Cdr. C.B. Crouch, DSO and Bar, RN), were escorted in by HMS Hebe (Lt.Cdr. G. Mowatt, RD, RNR) which had joined around 0845 A/24. (5)

6 Mar 1943 At 1500 A/6, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 13th war patrol (12th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Gulf of Hammamet, west of 12°00'E and between 35°25'N and 36°15'E.
En-route to her patrol area she was to proceed through position 35°41'N, 12°44'E.
For the daily and attack positions of HMS Unruffled during this patrol see the map below.

8 Mar 1943 P.M., HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) commenced to patrol to the north-east of Sousse. Each night she withdrew to seaward to charge as there were three powerful searchlights sweeping the sea intermittently in the vicinity of Sousse. (5)

11 Mar 1943 At 1930 A/11, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) left her current patrol area to proceed to an area enclosed by the following positions; 1) 37°05'N, 11°25'E, 2) 37°05'N, 11°59'E, 3) 36°12'N, 11°25'E and 4) 36°12'N, 10°16'E. This in accordance with the orders in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1123 A/11.
In a signal timed 1145 A/11, the Capt. S.10 had sent the following orders to HMS Unruffled; On arrival in your new area concentrate your patrol in the north-east corner. (38)
15 Mar 1943 At 0815 A/15, when in position 36°52'N, 11°30'E, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) sighted smoke bearing 050°. Lt. Stevens endeavoured to close.
At 0822 A/15, the smoke faded and was not seen again. (5)
16 Mar 1943 HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) sank a petrol lighter with a demolition charge between Nabeul and Korba, Tunisia.
0730 A/16 - Sighted a barge bearing 230°. Closed. It was later seen to be a tank lighter.
0945 A/16 - A party of Arabs boarded the lighter from a small boat and looted it. It was decided to deal with this lighter after dark as she was close inshore.
1930 A/16 - Surfaced. Set course to the north to find the lighter.
2012 A/16 - Went alongside the lighter in position 36°30'N, 10°53'E. A boarding party of two was sent over. A demolition charge was placed by them.
2032 A/16 - The charge exploded and the lighter sank. (5)

17 Mar 1943 At 2000 A/17, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN), which had conducted a periscope reconnaissance of Hammamet during the day, left patrol and set course to return to Malta as had been ordered in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1245 A/17 which had contained the following orders; Leave patrol as necessary to return to Malta and make rendezvous with the minesweeper at 0830 A/19. Proceed through the south-east corner of your patrol area thence between Linosa and Lampedusa. (39)
19 Mar 1943 Around 1230 A/19, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) ended her 13th war patrol (12th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
[It appears she was not escorted in by a minesweeper.] (5)

4 Apr 1943 Around 1600 B/4, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 14th war patrol (13th in the Mediterranean). She is ordered to perform a special operation off the south-east coast of Sicily (beach reconnaissance for the upcoming Operation Husky).
On board were two officers and one other ranks from C.O.P.P. party IV.
Before sailing some exercises had been carried out with the party members.
5 Apr 1943 At 2230 B/5, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) launched a Folbot for a practive run. The Folbot returned to the submarine 45 minutes later. (5)
6 Apr 1943 At 1200 B/6, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) commenced to conduct a periscope depth of several beaches.
At 2015 B/6, HMS Unruffled surfaced and examined the coast for leading marks. A moderate swell from the southwards precluded Folbot operations. (5)
7 Apr 1943 HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) continued her periscope reconnaissance of the beaches on the south-east coast of Sicily.
At 0825 B/7, a tug was sighted to the northward. This tug was later seen to be towing targets which were engaged at a range of about 6000 yards by shore batteries near Cape Ognina.
At 1145 B/7, the tug stopped 3000 yards from HMS Unruffled while she was shortening in her tow. On completion of which, at 1200 B/7, she made off for Syracuse.
At 2030 B/7, HMS Unruffled surfaced. The weather seemed suitable for Folbot operations.
At 2045 B/7, HMS Unruffled dived with the intention to launch a Folbot at 2245 B/7.
At 2230 B/7, HMS Unruffled surfaced.
At 2255 B/7, HMS Unruffled launched a Folbot but she returned 20 minutes later as the weather conditions were deteriorating. (5)
8 Apr 1943 At 2030 B/8, on surfacing, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) found the weather conditions unsuitable for Folbot operations.

Between 2225 B/8 and 2300 B/8, shore batteries near Avola carried out a shoot at a target that was illuminated by a searchlight. At that moment HMS Unruffled was charging her batteries 10 miles from the coast. (5)
9 Apr 1943 At 2030 B/8, on surfacing, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) found the weather conditions unsuitable for Folbot operations. Course was set to proceed to the Catania area. (5)

10 Apr 1943 Between 0700 B/10 and 0830 B/10, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) conducted a periscope reconnaissance of Catania Bay on completion of which the submarine withdrew to the south-east.
At 1325 B/10, the funnel and bridge of a torpedo boat was sighted inshore near Cape Santa Croce. The enemy vessel was northbound and presumably came from Syracuse.

At 2030 B/10, HMS Unruffled surfaced and set course to return to Malta. If the weather had been suitable for Folbot operations an attempt would have been made to reconnoitre the Cape Negro area but this was not the case. (5)
12 Apr 1943 Around 1030 B/12, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) ended her 14th war patrol (13th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (5)

28 Apr 1943 Around 1500 B/28, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 15th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean). She is ordered to perform a special operation (beach reconnaissance for the upcoming Operation Husky).
On board were one officer and one other ranks from C.O.P.P. party IV.
On departure an exercise was carried out with aircraft.
Between 2135 B/28 and 2220 B/28, a Folbot launching and recovery exercise was carried out.

30 Apr 1943 During daylight, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) conducted a periscope reconnaissance of the coast / beaches. Poor visibility and a rising north-east wind prevented Folbot operations being carried out during the night. This remained the same for 1, 2 and 3 May 1943. (5)

4 May 1943 At 0101 B/4, south of Cape Murro di Proco, Sicily, in position 36°45'N, 15°21'E, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) heard HE bearing Green 20° and a minute later the bow wave of a motor torpedo boat was seen approaching. HMS Unruffled dived to evade this enemy vessel.
At 0115 B/4, the HE faded after the motor torpedo boat had initially passed close down the port side. The enemy was steering a course of approximately 220°. (5)
5 May 1943 During 5 and 6 May 1943, bad visibility prevented HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) from carrying out inshore operations. (5)
7 May 1943 On 7 May 1943, strong wind prevented HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) from carrying out inshore operations. (5)

8 May 1943 At 2330 B/8, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN), launched a folbot for beach reconnaissance near Cape Murro di Porco. The operation had been delayed for days due to the unsuitable weather conditions. HMS Unruffled dived after having launched the Folbot.

At 0209 B/8, the explosions (signal charges) half a minute apart were heard. This was the pre-arranged signal in case the Folbot would return early and wished the submarine to close the rendezvous position to pick her up.
At 0215 B/8, HMS Unruffled surfaced. The Folbot was soon sighted an re-embarked.
At 0220 B/8, HMS Unruffled retired to seawards to charge.
At 1300 B/8, HMS Unruffled closed the shore near Cape Ognina for a periscope reconnaissance of the beach in this area as the Folbot had not found the beach last night.
At 1330 B/8, two motor torpedo boats passed astern on course 220°, apparently on passage.
At 2100 B/8, HMS Unruffled surfaced. The weather was found to be unsuitable for further Folbot operations. Lt. Stanley decided to return to Malta. (5)
11 May 1943 Around 1100 B/11, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) ended her 15th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (5)
25 May 1943 At 1715 A/25, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 16th war patrol (15th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the north of Messina.
She was escorted out until 2045 B/25 by HMS Hebe (Lt.Cdr. G. Mowatt, RD, RNR).
HMS Unruffled was routed through positions 36°08'N, 14°22'E thence 37°07'N, 13°15'E to pass position 'P' at 1200 B/27. The patrol area would be signalled later by the Capt. S.8.
27 May 1943 At 0300 A/25, south-west of Marsala, Sicily, in position 37°37'N, 12°14'E, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) sighted a submarine proceeding on an opposite course.

This is considered to have been HrMs Dolfijn (Lt.Cdr. H.M.L.F.E. van Oostrom Soede, RNN) which also sighted a vessel at the same time which was thought to be a motor torpedo boat but obviously was HMS Unruffled.
Both submarines were aware of each others presence through signals which had been received. (5)

28 May 1943 At 2330 B/28, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.8's signal timed 1418 B/28 in which she was ordered to patrol in area 'F'. [We have been unable to find the exact text of this signal.] (5)
29 May 1943 At 1340 B/29, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) arrived in area 'F'.
At 1415 B/29, off Cape Tindari, an F-boat was seen proceeding westwards on course 075°.
At 1600 B/29, three barges, which were at anchor off Tindari were examined following which the submarine retired to the northward.

At 2150 B/29, in position 38°21'N, 15°10'E, the Asdic reported HE bearing Green 160°. Shortly afterwards a white light appeared fairly high on the same bearing. HMS Unruffled dived and the HE was subsequently classified as being from a turbine doing 140 revs. No Asdic transmissions were heard. By 2200 B/29 the HE had faded to the westwards. (5)

30 May 1943 Around 0900 B/30, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) commenced to patrol off Cape Vaticano. Nothing was sighted all day and the submarine withdrew to the north-east at night for charging. (5)
31 May 1943 At 1440 B/31, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) sighted smoke to the northward. A Cant. Z.501 was circling overhead the smoke. Lt. Stevens closed Cape Vaticano to intercept.

At 1500 B/31, an attack was commenced on a small merchant vessel that was being escorted by a Generali-class torpedo boat. The attack however failed due to the enemy's constant alterations of course and the submarine did not get closer than 6000 yards. (5)

1 Jun 1943 During the day, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) patrolled about 3,5 miles from the coast to the northward of Cape Suvero. During the day only fishing craft, one motor torpedo boat and one or two other small craft were seen close inshore. (5)

2 Jun 1943 At 0730 B/2, on coming up to periscope depth after attempted repairs to the high power periscope, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) sighted that two merchant vessels in ballast had passed to the northward. They were escorted by a torpedo boat and a seaplane. By the time they were sighted they were well out of range. While the submarine had been deep for the repairs no HE had been picked up.
During the day the H.P. periscope completely fogged up and could no longer be used. (5)

3 Jun 1943 HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) torpedoed and sank the German tanker Henry Desprez (former French, 9895 GRT, built 1932) about 70 nautical miles north-east of Messina in position 39°13'N, 16°01'E. She was en-route from Naples to Taranto escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Clio.
The Italian torpedo boat Sirtori, the Italian A/S motorboat VAS 217 and the German auxiliary submarine chaser UJ 2212 sailed from Messina to hunt the submarine.

1510 B/3 - Sighted a southbound vessel and an escorting torpedo boat 5000 yards away. An attack was commenced. The vessel was identified as a medium sized modern type tanker and the escort was identified as a new modern class torpedo boat.
1531 B/3 - Fired four torpedoes from 1100 yards. Three torpedo hits were heard.
1539 B/3 - Unruffled was counter-attacked by the escort, with, in all, fifteen depth-charges. No damage to the submarine was done. After the attack was over Unruffled withdrew to the west. (5)
4 Jun 1943 At 0930 B/4, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) left patrol to return to Malta in accordance with the signal timed 1352 B/2 and the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1115 B/3. (5)
8 Jun 1943 Around 1100 B/8, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) ended her 16th war patrol (15th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (5)

24 Jun 1943 At 1830 B/24, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 17th war patrol (16th in the Mediterranean). She is ordered to perform a special operation with a party from C.O.P.P. V (beach reconnaissance for the upcoming Operation Husky).
The C.O.P.P. party was made up of three officers and two other ranks.
[No log is available for this period so no map can be displayed.] (40)
25 Jun 1943 During daylight hours, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) conducted a periscope reconnaissance of the beaches.
At 2200 B/25, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN), launched a Folbot for beach reconnaissance of '45' beach. The Folbot returned at 0140 B/26. (40)
26 Jun 1943 At 2200 B/26, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN), launched a Folbot for beach reconnaissance of '47' beach. The Folbot returned at 0255 B/27. (40)
27 Jun 1943 At 2200 B/27, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN), launched a Folbot for beach reconnaissance of '48A' beach. The Folbot returned at 0230 B/28.
HMS Unruffled then set course to conduct a periscope reconnaissance of beaches in the vicinity of Cape Marzamemi on completion of which course was set to return to Malta. (40)
29 Jun 1943 Around 1100 B/29, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) ended her 17th war patrol (16th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (40)

6 Jul 1943 At 2000 B/6, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 18th war patrol (17th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to perform a special operation during operation Husky. She was to lay three buoys and launch two Folbots to act as marking positions near Cape Murro di Porco for the Husky landings (invasion of Sicily).
The submarine was routed through positions 040° - St. Elmo light - 20 miles and 36°34'N, 15°26'E thence course 055°. Speed of advance, 9 knots.
[No log is available for this period so no map can be displayed.] (41)
8 Jul 1943 In the evening HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) laid three buoys near Cape Murro di Porco, Sicily for the upcoming operation Husky, the landings in Sicily.
2150 B/8 - Laid one type FH 830 in position 232°, Cape Murro di Porco, 5.75 nautical miles.
2220 B/8 - Laid a second type FH 830 in position 232°, Cape Murro di Porco, 7.75 nautical miles.
2315 B/8 - Laid a third type FH 830 in position 229°, Cape Murro di Porco, 9.7 nautical miles. (42)
9 Jul 1943 In the evening, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) launched two Folbots near Cape Murro di Porco, Sicily.
2145 B/9 - Launched a Folbot for 'George sector' manned by a crew of two.
2220 B/9 - Launched a second Folbot, but now for 'Jig sector' also manned by a crew of two.
2305 B/9- In position 183 degrees, Cape Murro di Porco lighthouse, 6 nautical miles and started transmitting. The convoy was sighted coming in at 0001/10.
0040 B/10 - Stopped transmitting.

0650 B/10 - Proceeded southwards. HMS Unseen (Lt. M.L.C. Crawford, DSC and Bar, RN) was now in company. They were joined by the trawler HMS Kerrera (A/Skr.Lt. R.W. Slater, RNR) for onward escort to Malta at 0730 B/10. The trawler remained in company with the submarines until 1230 B/10. (42)
10 Jul 1943 At 1500 B/10, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) ended her 18th war patrol (17th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (42)
27 Jul 1943 At 2000 B/27, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 19th war patrol (18th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Gulf of Taranto.

She was routed as follows; Proceed by the eastern swept channel and then through positions 36°02'N, 16°30'E at 2000 B/28, 37°16'N, 18°01'E at 1030 B/29th and 38°34'N, 18°01'E at 2000 B/29 and then course 360° to establish patrol in the Gulf of Taranto.
30 Jul 1943 At 0600 B/30, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) established patrol in the vicinity of position 39°48'N, 18°00'E.

At 0735 B/30, two destroyers passed. Enemy course was 315°, speed 10 knots. At 2345 B/30, HMS Unruffled set course to comply with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1858 B/30 which had contained the following orders; Proceed forthwith to enter Adriatic routed along 18°52'E to 40°40'N. thence to patrol within arc of radius 15 miles from Brindisi. (43)

31 Jul 1943 At 2237 B/31, in the Otranto Strait, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) sighted a large two-funnelled hospital ship proceeding on course 140°. [No exact position is given in the patrol report and no log is available for this month.] (5)

1 Aug 1943 HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) attacked the Italian transport Città di Catania (3355 GRT, built 1910) with one torpedo north-east of Brindisi, Italy. The torpedo missed its target.
0400 B/1 - Established patrol 6 miles off shore in the vicinity of Brindisi.
0622 B/1 - Sighted a large vessel outward bound on a course of 050°. Range was 8000 yards. This proved to be a three-funnelled transport, unescorted, speed 3 knots. Started attack.
0639 B/1 - In position 40°46'N, 18°07'E fired one torpedo from 2500 yards. It missed, presumably due to the enemy yawing.
1115 B/1 - A motor torpedo boat passed at speed on course 045°.
1145 B/1 - Two small merchant vessels in ballast and one torpedo boat passed to the seaward. They were northbound.
1540 B/1 - A small merchant vessel in ballast, which was towing two schooners, approached from the south and entered Brindisi on a course of 230°. (5)

2 Aug 1943 At 1000 B/2, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) commenced an attack on a vessel seen approaching from the south. The attack was broken off when the target was seen to be an old tramp of about 1000 tons which was in ballast.
At 1945 B/2, a tug towing two gunnery targets was seen leaving Brindisi. (5)
3 Aug 1943 HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian transport Città di Catania (3355 GRT, built 1910) north-east of Brindisi in position 40°44'N, 18°05'E.

The Italian vessel was on passage from Durazzo for Brindisi. Rescue operations were organised very quickly as a number of patrol vessels were near by sweeping the area. The pilot vessel Galliano, the minesweeper RD 32 and the fishing vessels Immacolata, Caterina, Nuovo Francesco and San Rocco. The number of casualties and survivors varies according to sources but about 259 were killed or missing and about 242 survivors were picked up. The corvette Scimittara, two motorboats and a VAS A/S boat were sailed to hunt the submarine.
0545 B/3 - A large floating crane, towed by two tugs and escorted by an armed merchant cruiser all passed on a steady course of 050°, speed 5 knots. It is thought they were bound for Durazzo.
1035 B/3 - A large vessel loomed out of the mist, bearing 040°, range 8000 yards. This turned out to be the same three-funnelled transport that we attacked on 1st August. Commenced attack.
1054 B/3 - In position 40°44'N, 18°05'E fired a salvo of four torpedoes from 1000 yards. Three hits were heard.
1056 B/3 - Almost the whole of the enemy ship were obscured by dense smoke and flame. She was settling by the stern.
1057 B/3 - The ship sank stern first. About 100 survivors were seen in the water. Went deep and withdrew to the north-north-east. (5)

5 Aug 1943 At 2115 B/5, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) set course to proceed to patrol off Valona in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1215 B/2 which contained the following orders; Leave Brindisi area after dark/5 and proceed upon patrol off the southern approach to Valona outside 200 fathom line by daylight/6 keeping clear of HMS Unshaken (Lt. J. Whitton, RN). Leaving patrol to pass through positions 40°15'N, 19°13'E at 0500 B/7, 38°47'N, 18°28'E at 0800 B/3, 35°44'N, 16°39'E at 1200 B/9 to make rendezvous with the minesweeper the seaward end of the searched channel at 0800 B/10. Proceed on the surface on the 8th. (44)
6 Aug 1943 At 0430 B/6, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) dived and commenced to patrol in the vicinity of position 230° - Cape Garlovez - 5 miles.
At 0740 B/6, the masts and funnels of a three-funnelled torpedo boat was seen proceeding southwards up to the Valona anchorage. Also the masts of three schooners and a trawler could be seen.
At 0900 B/6, two schooners which had swept in the area were seen to return to harbour. Also the masts of a stationary schooner were seen to the north of Saseno Island. (5)
7 Aug 1943 At 0500 B/7, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) left patrol to return to Malta. (5)
10 Aug 1943Át 1200 B/10, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) ended her 19th war patrol (18th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (5)

22 Aug 1943 At 1430 B/22, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 20th war patrol (19th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to perform a special operation (Operation 'Seaman') and to patrol off Brindisi.
The submarine was escorted out by HMS BYMS 2028 (Skr. J.R. Clark, RNR).

HMS Unruffled was routed via estern swept channel and then through positions 35°50'N, 16°35'E at 1200 B/23, 37°58'N, 19°39'E at 2000 B/24 thence to operate for 24 hours off Cape Dukato for the special operation. The submarine was to proceed on the surface on the 24th.
25 Aug 1943 Around 2045 B/25, from position 230° - Cape Vliotis - 2.3 miles, HMS Unruffled (Lt. J.S. Stevens, DSO, DSC, RN) launched a collapsible RAF dinghy to land two Greek officers on Cephalonia.
The operation was completed at 2134 B/25, when the submarine set course for her patrol area.
